main_summary.summary: Maria Varela, born January 1, 1940 in Newell, PA to a Mexican father from Mazapil, Zacatecas and an Irish-German mother, recounts her path from Catholic social-action organizing through SNCC field work and into the Chicano land grant movement. Interviewed June 29, 2016 in Pasadena by David Cline and Guha Shankar, she traces her formation in the Young Christian Students at Alverno College, her exposure to SNCC organizers Charles McDew and Tim Jenkins at National Student Association congresses, her participation in the 1962 Port Huron Conference with Tom Hayden and Al Haber, and Casey Hayden's recruitment of her to SNCC's Atlanta office. Sent to Selma in 1963 to support Edmundite priest Maurice Ouellet at St. Elizabeth Mission, Varela developed adult literacy programs and was arrested under Jim Clark's enforcement of city ordinances against the July 1964 Civil Rights Act. She designed photo-illustrated literacy booklets featuring the Panola County Okra Co-op, learned documentary photography from Matt Herron, and became one of nine SNCC photographers, covering the James Meredith March Against Fear. After 1966 she worked with Reies López Tijerina's Alianza in New Mexico, the Poor People's Campaign, and ultimately founded Tierra Wools, a Churro sheep livestock and weaving cooperative preserving Hispanic and Navajo land-based economies.
key_themes: SNCC adult literacy and movement publications
Documentary photography in the civil rights movement
Chicano land grant movement and rural cooperatives
main_summary.historical_significance:
Varela is one of nine SNCC staff photographers and the only Latina among them; her interview documents the bridge between the Black freedom movement and the emergent Chicano movement, including the Poor People's Campaign multicultural delegation. She provides primary-source testimony correcting the public narrative that SNCC expelled non-Black staff after 1966, citing a personalized letter from Stokely Carmichael and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson instructing her to continue her work with Fannie Lou Hamer.

Her account of producing photo-illustrated co-op booklets, film strips, and a documentary on César Chávez's United Farm Workers for distribution in Mississippi captures a less-documented dimension of SNCC's grassroots organizing infrastructure. Her later founding of clinics, co-ops, and Tierra Wools in northern New Mexico extends the empowerment model she learned in SNCC into Hispanic land grant communities, preserving the Churro sheep breed and Spanish-language weaving traditions.

chapters.title: Born in a Blizzard in Newell, PA
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline and Guha Shankar in Pasadena in June 2016, Maria Varela introduces herself as born January 1, 1940, in a blizzard in Newell, Pennsylvania, and describes a family that was not political but socially open on injustice, a scrupulously fair mother, and a father who had known racism after coming from Mexico.

chapters.title: Greaser, Go Home, and the Scholarships
chapters.summary: Varela recounts her father being born in Mazapil, Zacatecas, coming to the United States around age nine during the Mexican Revolution into fierce anti-immigrant sentiment, her grandfather choosing to settle where there were few Mexicanos, and her father and his brothers winning football scholarships, playing harder for every epithet thrown at them.

chapters.title: The Secretary-Until-a-Husband Track
chapters.summary: She describes hunting trips with her father and the shotgun she later took south, the Catholic high school's terminal-education track in shorthand and typing aimed at secretarial work until marriage, how a family friend dropping dead and leaving a widow with five children changed her father's mind and led her to Alverno College in Milwaukee, her grandfather's ban on the boys speaking Spanish, and her Irish and German mother whose father raised money for the IRA.

chapters.title: Society Has to Be Restructured for Justice
chapters.summary: She describes the era when social engagement was treated as the core of Catholicism, the social inquiry movement and Christian Family Movement rooted in the Belgian worker priest movement and Pope Leo XIII's encyclical, and the see-judge-act training method of finding a problem by asking the people affected, then reflecting and acting.

chapters.title: Out of the Catholic Ghetto
chapters.topic: Young Christian Students and the NSA
chapters.summary: Varela recalls joining the Young Christian Students in high school and college, an action-oriented method that always demanded a next step, and its thrust to get out of the Catholic ghetto and into the National Student Association, which the nuns suspected of communism in the post-McCarthy era.

chapters.title: They Wanted the NSA on Record
chapters.summary: As student body president working the National Student Association Secretariat with her typing skills, Varela heard SNCC sit-in participants press the NSA to support the sit-ins against a hostile bloc of Southern schools, was struck by how articulately organizers like Charles McDew and Tim Jenkins defeated every argument, and met Tom Hayden building the liberal caucus that became SDS, marveling that relationships drew a girl who had never read C. Wright Mills or Marx into that world.

chapters.title: Rat-Infested Apartments on the West Side
chapters.summary: She recounts postponing a Marquette speech therapy fellowship to accept a Young Christian Students national staff offer, moving to Chicago headquarters to live on seven dollars a week in rat-infested West Side apartments, and Tom Hayden reaching her to help put together a manifesto at the Port Huron conference.

chapters.title: Why Do We Need Adult Overseers
chapters.summary: Varela explains that student groups were then appendages of adult organizations, making SNCC's independence remarkable, as Diane Nash said they had brought Nashville to its knees, and recalls meeting Casey Hayden at the Port Huron conference, typing the original minutes, and later getting Casey's letter that the SNCC office desperately needed help.

chapters.title: You're Pretty Hypocritical
chapters.summary: Varela describes the semantic and authenticity questions she helped resolve in the Port Huron statement, Casey Hayden coming out of the Young Christian Women, and her months of procrastination before deciding it was hypocritical to exhort Catholic students to support the movement while refusing the SNCC office's request that she come to Atlanta.

chapters.title: When Lightning Strikes, It Illuminates
chapters.topic: A Summer School and the Selma Offer
chapters.summary: She recounts the six-week summer school that NSA, Connie Curry, and SNCC ran to train young activists being jailed and beaten in Southern towns, where Robert Coles taught and she first learned Afro-American history, an exploding epiphany she likens to lightning illuminating everything and watched transform how young Black men carried themselves, and how Frank Smith and the badly beaten Bernard Lafayette Jr. then asked her, to her surprise, what she would think about going to Selma.

chapters.title: A Priest, a Mission, and a Literacy Test
chapters.summary: She explains being recruited to support a French Canadian priest at St. Elizabeth Mission, an Afro-American Catholic church served by the Edmundites whose hospital and school employed the Black community, who wanted literacy training so people could take the rigged twenty-one-question voter registration test, with thoughts of a possible class-action lawsuit.

chapters.title: We Are All American Here
chapters.summary: Asked about her own identity, Varela reflects that her family moved too much to grow up in a Mexican community and spoke no Spanish at home though her father was proud of his heritage, and recalls a nun who, hearing her mention being Mexican, walked down the aisle to declare that they were all American here, a wound she carried for years.

chapters.title: Maybe Adults Already Could Read
chapters.summary: Varela describes teaching a woman who wanted to read the Bible and observing, through the YCS method of watching and reflecting, that adult non-readers already had varied vocabulary tied to what they wanted to know, and learning that foundations were eager to fund literacy work tied to voter education in the movement.

chapters.title: Four Black Kids and a Thousand White Kids
chapters.summary: She recounts Rennie Davis writing her first funded proposal, recruiting four to six Afro-American students to develop literacy materials in Selma for the summer of 1964, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson pointing out the contrast with Bob Moses bringing a thousand white students to Mississippi, a program Varela thought was a horrible idea given how hard it was just to keep her few students safe.

chapters.title: Here's Jim Clark With His Cattle Prod
chapters.summary: Varela tells how, the day after the Civil Rights Act was signed on July 2, 1964, her students went to the Thirsty Boy to be served because it was the law of the land, only for Jim Clark to haul them to jail with his cattle prod, leaving her four weeks raising bail and dodging a warrant under a Selma ordinance banning meetings, and reflects that Selma, drawing students mostly from the North, was like a Nazi state with none of the refuge Mississippi offered.

chapters.part: Books That Showed People Making Change
chapters.title: It's Bigger Than CAT, C-A-T
chapters.summary: Varela describes shifting literacy toward what people actually wanted to read, working with Afro-American farmers in Batesville, Panola County, on an okra co-op to make booklets and filmstrips showing Black people in leadership, materials so prized that an organizer begged her not to pass them out before meetings, and how the hunger for education brought out parents who skipped voter registration but filled Freedom Schools that became Head Start, work she likens to a second Reconstruction that SNCC alone had boots on the ground to do.

chapters.title: A Darkroom in a Shotgun Shack
chapters.summary: Varela tells of moving to a shotgun shack near Tougaloo College and building a darkroom with Bobby Fletcher because the books needed photographs of Black people that no files held, being sent to learn from Matt Herron, a New Orleans commercial photographer who taught SNCC people the gray-scale documentary method passed down from a student of Ansel Adams, and the SNCC photographers' duty to cover events as an extra eye that might protect marchers, as she did at the James Meredith March Against Fear.

chapters.title: To Show the Local Folks, Not the Celebrities
chapters.summary: Varela describes the nine SNCC photographers as the only photography department of any civil rights organization, their purpose to show local leaders rather than celebrities, the This Light of Ours exhibit, and how the camera simply expressed her vision since she already lived in the Black community, later shooting Latino participants in the Poor People's Campaign.

chapters.title: Are You Pregnant? It Will Spoil the Hog
chapters.summary: Varela recounts producing Fannie Lou Hamer's first autobiography with Julius Lester and helping her campaign after Atlantic City, the day Hamer met her at the door asking whether she or her sister was pregnant lest it spoil the hog they were cutting, their conversations over chicharrones about the kinship of Mexican and Black flavors, the poor white families Hamer quietly supplied from donation trucks, and Hamer telling the 1964 debate she would not be part of an organization that did not reflect the society they wanted.

chapters.title: You Could Make Twenty-Five a Day
chapters.topic: Filmstrips and the United Farm Workers
chapters.summary: Varela explains the filmstrip printer New York supporters built, the cheap storyboarded strips with scripts and canvas mailing bags so organizers anywhere could show them, being asked by the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union to document Cesar Chavez's United Farm Workers, and how Marshall Ganz and George Ballis got the SNCC body to support the union with surplus Plymouth Valiants and CB radios, making SNCC the first Afro-American civil rights organization to back the farm workers.

chapters.title: We're Not the Only Ones
chapters.topic: A Filmstrip in a Tent City
chapters.summary: Varela tells of an Afro-American Catholic priest from northern Louisiana who gathered plantation workers thrown off the land for trying to vote, where she showed the Panola County okra filmstrip and the farm workers strip, moving an older man to tears that they were not the only ones the sheriffs beat, and admits the mortal flaw that the strips assumed projectors the poor Black schools did not have.

chapters.title: Keep Us Informed
chapters.summary: Varela dismantles the narrative that non-Black people were thrown out of SNCC, noting John Lewis was hurt at not being re-elected chair but the story was blown up in the media, that two months after Stokely Carmichael was elected and SNCC adopted an all-Black executive committee he and Ruby Doris sent her a personalized letter encouraging her work with Mrs. Hamer's campaign, and that Bob Zellner was simply told SNCC lacked resources for white-community work.

chapters.title: It Was a Strategy, Not a Creed
chapters.summary: She explains that those who came in by late 1962 or 1963, herself included, were tactically rather than philosophically nonviolent, that she brought a gun to defend herself, that after Atlantic City and the murder of three civil rights workers a deep anger and the lesson of Black history's effect on identity led many to insist they control their own organization, and that foundations then cut her support because she would not repudiate black power, leaving Elizabeth Betita Martinez running the New York office.
related_events: murder of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

chapters.title: A Bridge to the Land Grant Movement
chapters.summary: Varela recounts Julian Bond inviting her in the fall of 1966 to host a New Mexico land grant leader known for a courthouse raid at the National Conference for New Politics, his later letter asking her to come work as a bridge between the civil rights and land grant movements, and her choice of New Mexico over offers in Crystal City and with Cesar Chavez because farmers with their own land could not be thrown off it, reflecting on how much Black land had been lost since Reconstruction.

chapters.title: Protected Only by a Treaty
chapters.summary: She describes taking a seven-dollar-a-week job, the land grant movement reaching back to the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo and the only non-Native people protected by treaty, and watching elderly members in neat worn clothes and battered hats slip the charismatic leader hundreds of dollars from selling cattle, prompting her to wonder who was actually organizing it.

chapters.title: No Room in Resurrection City
chapters.topic: Bunk Beds in a Borrowed School
chapters.summary: Varela recounts being handed the Poor People's Campaign logistics, going to Washington where there was no room in the swampy Resurrection City, so the women were housed with bunk beds in a borrowed school with communal meals, and wondering how the SCLC people carried on so soon after King's assassination.

chapters.title: Chicanos Don't Put Up With That
chapters.summary: She describes the SCLC running roughshod over everyone on its crusade, so the Chicanos staged their own actions at the State, Education, and Justice Departments over the treaty, racism in education, and police brutality, the Puerto Ricans leading everyone across the bridge to Robert Kennedy's grave, the Young Lords and Brown Berets uniting on security, an Appalachian contingent the women called poorer than themselves, and the experienced Rodolfo Gonzales acting as de facto leader.

chapters.title: Thirty-Three Chicano Newspapers
chapters.summary: She describes a Department of Education hearing in Washington where people testified about school conditions, returning to work on the Chicano Press Association linking thirty-three papers with a photo and news service, writing for Chicano newspapers, and meeting Anselmo Tijerina, brother of the land grant leader, who believed the people themselves should do the work in Tierra Amarilla.

chapters.title: A Call at Three in the Morning
chapters.summary: Varela tells of Anselmo's plan to work the land with potatoes and a hog farm rather than just talk about it, his determination to buy a clinic after the doctor left for the city because women were giving birth on the highway, and the three a.m. call that the clinic had been torched by Anglo bankers and ranchers fearing it would legitimize the land grant movement.

chapters.title: Mississippi All Over Again
chapters.summary: She recounts deciding she had to go after the arson, the Presbyterian Church taking out the mortgage on a clinic still operating since 1969, and framing thirty years of New Mexico work as a translation of what black power and SNCC meant to her, including work with livestock growers and weavers to preserve a nearly extinct breed of sheep the USDA had condemned and to build businesses from its meat and wool, while learning how reluctant the country is to support rural people empowering themselves.

chapters.part: Land, Power, and a Legacy Carried Forward
chapters.title: We Study SNCC, They Told Me
chapters.topic: From Land Economics to a New Generation
chapters.summary: Varela reflects on the saying that the white people got the money and the brown people got the politics while money in the rural West and black belt South is rooted in land, then turns to her amazement at the undocumented student movement, its nine hundred-strong Houston meeting and California victories on in-state tuition, and students telling her they study SNCC as the closest of the movements to them for its anti-celebrity, team-based values.

chapters.part: Land, Power, and a Legacy Carried Forward
chapters.title: It's Bigger Than a Hamburger
chapters.topic: A Legacy Written Out of History
chapters.summary: She recounts the undocumented movement widening from individual relief to include families, echoing Fannie Lou Hamer's line that it was bigger than a hamburger, calls these young people heroes carrying the best of SNCC's legacy, and notes she taught SNCC as a case history for sixteen years to students asking why they never learned it in school.

chapters.part: Land, Power, and a Legacy Carried Forward
chapters.title: Even If It Takes Fifty Years
chapters.summary: Varela recalls Ella Baker urging the movement to reflect after the 1964 convention, the setback when the undocumented movement nearly won DACA much as SNCC lost the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party fight in 1964, and closes that the best of a movement carries into a later generation if the empowerment strategies are solid, as the interview ends with her daughter Sabina Zuniga Varela present.

loc_video.caption: Maria Varela oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Pasadena, California, 2016 June 29
